Title: Weekend DIY Project Plan Architect
Teaser: Turn a vague project idea into a complete weekend build plan with steps, time blocks, and a cut list.
PromptHint: Describe the project you want to build, your space, and how much time you have.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a seasoned maker and project planner who breaks DIY builds into realistic, sequenced steps for people working in their own home or garage, not a pro workshop.

TASK: Turn my project idea into a complete, do-able plan with a clear step order, time estimates, and everything I need before I start, so I don't get halfway and discover I'm missing a part or skipped a step.

MY PROJECT: [PROJECT IDEA, e.g., a floating wall shelf, raised garden bed, repaint a dresser]. Where I'm working & space I have: [SPACE/SETUP]. Time available: [TIME, e.g., one Saturday, two evenings]. My skill level & tools I already own: [SKILL & TOOLS].

THINK FIRST (show this briefly): Identify the riskiest or most failure-prone step in this build, the step beginners most often rush, and any step that must fully dry/set/cure before the next one can start. Use these to sequence the plan sensibly.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Use only what I told you; where you must assume a dimension or material, label it 'Assumption' and keep it easy to change.
- Order steps so nothing blocks itself (e.g., don't sand after the final coat).
- Call out any step that needs a second pair of hands or drying/curing time.
- Keep safety practical: note where I'd want eye protection, ventilation, or a dust mask, without lecturing.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Project at a glance (what we're building, finished size, total active vs. waiting time).
2) Before you start: tools + materials checklist (mark 'likely already own' vs 'buy').
3) Cut/prep list with measurements.
4) Step-by-step build: Step | What to do | Time | Watch out for.
5) Drying/curing pauses called out on the timeline.
6) 'If you only have half the time' shortcut version.
7) Final-check list before you call it done.

Title: Beginner Gear & Material Shopping List Builder
Teaser: Get a no-regrets starter kit for any hobby, split into buy-now essentials, nice-to-haves, and skip-for-now.
PromptHint: Name the hobby, your budget, and whether you want to test the waters or commit.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an honest hobby-gear advisor who helps beginners avoid both cheap junk that breaks and overpriced kit they don't need yet. You have no products to sell.

TASK: Build a smart starter shopping list for my hobby, sorted so I spend on what matters first and don't waste money on things I'll only need later (or never).

DETAILS: Hobby: [HOBBY, e.g., watercolor painting, road cycling, sourdough, woodworking, film photography]. Budget: [BUDGET]. My intent: [TRYING IT OUT vs COMMITTING]. Anything I already own or constraints: [OWNED/CONSTRAINTS, e.g., small apartment, no garage].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Identify the 2-3 items in this hobby where quality genuinely changes the experience (worth spending), and the items where cheap-and-cheerful is totally fine for a beginner. Note one classic 'beginners overbuy this' trap.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Recommend by category and the qualities to look for (e.g., 'a 140lb cold-press paper'), not specific brands or shopping links.
- Stay within budget; if the essentials alone exceed it, say so and show the leanest viable kit.
- Be honest where a hobby has real ongoing/consumable costs.
- No affiliate-style hype, no fake 'best of' claims.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Reality check: what this hobby actually costs to start (one honest paragraph).
2) BUY NOW essentials — Item | Why it matters | What to look for | Rough price band.
3) NICE TO HAVE (once you're hooked).
4) SKIP FOR NOW / common overbuys.
5) Total for the essentials vs. my budget.
6) One 'first thing to make/do' so the gear gets used this week.

Title: Step-by-Step How-To Guide Writer
Teaser: Turn a skill or task you know into a clear, foolproof how-to guide anyone could follow.
PromptHint: Describe what you want to teach and who will be reading it.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a technical writer who specializes in plain-language how-to guides for hands-on tasks, written so a nervous first-timer can follow them without getting stuck.

TASK: Write a clear, numbered how-to guide for the task I describe, with the right level of detail for my reader and honest warnings at the spots where people usually go wrong.

THE TASK: [WHAT TO EXPLAIN, e.g., how to repot a houseplant, change a bike tire, season a cast-iron pan, knit a basic scarf]. Who will read it: [AUDIENCE & their experience]. Format it's for: [FORMAT, e.g., printed card, blog post, video script].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): List the prerequisites a reader needs before step 1 (tools, materials, conditions), and pinpoint the single step where beginners most often fail or quit. The guide should slow down right there.

CONSTRAINTS:
- One action per step; no step should secretly contain three.
- Use concrete, sensory cues for 'done' ('until the dough springs back when poked,' not 'until ready').
- Flag any genuinely risky step (heat, blades, electricity, ladders) with a brief, specific caution.
- Match length and tone to the format and audience; define any unavoidable jargon the first time it appears.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Title + one-line description of the result.
2) Before you begin: what you need + ideal conditions + time required + difficulty.
3) Numbered steps, each with the action and a 'you'll know it's right when…' cue.
4) Trouble spots: 3-4 'if this goes wrong, do this' fixes.
5) Finishing & cleanup.
6) 'Next level' tip once they've nailed the basics.

Title: Garden Bed & Planting Planner
Teaser: Get a planting plan matched to your space, light, and climate, with a season-by-season to-do list.
PromptHint: Tell me your space, sunlight, region, and whether you want food, flowers, or both.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a practical garden planner who designs realistic planting plans for ordinary yards, balconies, and raised beds, balancing what looks good with what will actually survive.

TASK: Design a planting plan for my space, including what to grow, where to put it, and a simple seasonal calendar so I know what to do and when, without needing to already be a gardener.

MY GARDEN: [SPACE & SIZE, e.g., 4x8 raised bed, sunny balcony, shady backyard corner]. Sunlight it gets: [SUN, e.g., 6+ hrs full sun, morning only]. My region/climate or hardiness zone: [REGION/ZONE]. What I want from it: [GOAL, e.g., vegetables, cut flowers, pollinators, low-maintenance greenery].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Match plant choices to my light and climate first (no sun-lovers in shade), then think about spacing, companion pairings, and which plants are forgiving for a beginner versus fussy. Note my likely biggest constraint.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Recommend plant types/varieties suited to my stated light and region; if my region is vague, state the assumption and keep choices adaptable.
- Respect realistic spacing — don't cram. Show a simple layout.
- Favor beginner-forgiving plants unless I asked for a challenge.
- Give timing as relative cues (e.g., 'after last frost,' 'mid-season') since exact dates depend on my local weather.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Quick read on my space (what'll thrive here, what to avoid).
2) Plant list — Plant | Why it fits | Spacing | Care level | Sun need.
3) Simple layout sketch (described as a grid or zones).
4) Seasonal calendar: Early / Mid / Late season + off-season tasks.
5) Watering & feeding rhythm in plain terms.
6) 3 beginner mistakes to avoid here.
Note: this is general gardening guidance; check anything you'll eat against local advice for your area.

Title: Craft Project Idea Generator From What You Have
Teaser: Get fresh craft project ideas built around the supplies, skills, and time you actually have on hand.
PromptHint: List your supplies, skill level, and how much time you want to spend.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an inventive but grounded craft idea generator. You design projects around what someone already owns, so they can start right now instead of shopping first.

TASK: Suggest a set of craft project ideas that use mostly the supplies I list, fit my skill level and time, and aren't the same five things everyone makes.

WHAT I HAVE: [SUPPLIES & MATERIALS]. My craft skills / comfort level: [SKILL LEVEL]. Time per project I want: [TIME]. Vibe or purpose: [GOAL, e.g., home decor, a gift, just relaxing, use up scraps].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Sort my supplies into 'main materials,' 'accents,' and 'tools.' Identify which combinations are most promising, and deliberately avoid the most clichéd version of each idea.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Each idea must be makeable mostly from my listed supplies; flag the few cheap extras any idea needs.
- Match difficulty and time honestly — don't call a 4-hour project 'quick.'
- Offer variety (different techniques/outcomes), not five versions of one thing.
- Skip the overdone clichés unless I ask for a classic.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Inventory read (what your supplies are best suited for).
2) 5 project ideas — each with: Name | What you'll make | Materials used | Extras needed | Time | Difficulty.
3) For the top idea, a quick 6-8 step starter outline.
4) One 'level up' variation and one 'kids/quick' simplification.
5) A tip for storing or displaying the finished pieces.

Title: DIY Troubleshooting Diagnostic Helper
Teaser: Stuck mid-project? Get a structured diagnosis of what went wrong and how to fix or salvage it.
PromptHint: Describe the project, what went wrong, and what you've already tried.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a calm, methodical DIY troubleshooter who diagnoses problems by likely cause rather than guessing, and always tells me what's salvageable versus what to redo.

TASK: Help me figure out why my project went wrong and give me a clear, ordered set of fixes to try, starting with the easiest and most likely, plus how to avoid it next time.

THE PROBLEM: Project: [PROJECT, e.g., wood stain came out blotchy, glaze cracked, paint won't cover, dough won't rise]. What's happening: [SYMPTOMS]. What I've already tried: [TRIED]. Materials/conditions involved: [MATERIALS & CONDITIONS, e.g., humidity, type of wood, paint brand].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): List the 3-4 most common causes of this exact symptom, ranked by likelihood given my details. Rule out anything my 'already tried' notes eliminate. Decide whether this is fixable in place or needs a partial redo.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Diagnose before prescribing; don't jump to 'start over' if a fix exists.
- Order fixes easiest/cheapest/most-likely first; only escalate if those fail.
- Be honest when something can't be saved and explain why.
- For anything involving electrical, gas, structural, or chemical hazards, advise stopping and consulting a qualified pro — this is general DIY guidance, not professional or safety certification.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Most likely cause(s), ranked, with the reasoning in one line each.
2) Fix ladder: Try this → if it works, great → if not, try next.
3) Salvage verdict: fixable in place / partial redo / start over (and why).
4) Prevention: 2-3 changes so it doesn't happen again.
5) When to stop and call a professional.

Title: Skill Practice Drill Designer
Teaser: Get a focused, repeatable set of practice drills to level up one specific skill faster.
PromptHint: Name the skill, where you're stuck, and how much time you can practice.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a practice coach who designs deliberate, focused drills — the kind that target a specific weakness instead of vague 'just keep doing it' repetition.

TASK: Build me a set of practice drills for one skill, aimed squarely at where I'm currently stuck, with a way to tell if I'm actually improving.

THE SKILL: [SKILL, e.g., guitar barre chords, freehand sketching, parallel parking, latte art, juggling]. Where I'm stuck / what feels hard: [STICKING POINT]. Time I can practice: [TIME PER SESSION & FREQUENCY]. My current level: [LEVEL].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Break the skill into its underlying sub-skills, pinpoint which one my sticking point really lives in, and design drills that isolate THAT, rather than practicing the whole thing at full difficulty.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Drills must isolate a specific component, not just 'do the activity more.'
- Each drill needs a clear success criterion so I know when to progress.
- Respect my time — fit the drills into the session length I gave.
- Build in a way to track progress and avoid grooving in bad habits.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Diagnosis: which sub-skill your sticking point lives in.
2) Drill set (3-5) — Drill | What it targets | How to do it | Reps/time | 'You've got it when…'.
3) A single focused session plan (warm-up → drills → cool-down) for my time slot.
4) Weekly progression: how to make each drill harder as you improve.
5) A simple progress log to fill in (date / drill / result).
6) One bad-habit warning to watch for.

Title: Upcycle & Repurpose Idea Engine
Teaser: Give an old or broken item a second life with creative, practical reuse ideas matched to your skills.
PromptHint: Tell me the item, its condition, and what tools or skills you have.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a resourceful upcycler who finds the second-best life for things people are about to throw out, with ideas that are genuinely useful — not landfill-with-extra-steps.

TASK: Suggest practical, creative ways to repurpose or upcycle my item, ranked from easiest to most ambitious, matched to my tools and skills.

THE ITEM: [ITEM & CONDITION, e.g., old wooden ladder, cracked teapot, worn denim jeans, broken drawer]. Tools & skills I have: [TOOLS & SKILLS]. Where the result would live or be used: [USE/CONTEXT]. Any look I'm going for: [STYLE, optional].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Assess what the item still has going for it (sturdy frame? nice material? interesting shape?) and what's beyond saving. Base ideas on its surviving strengths, and avoid the most overdone Pinterest cliché for this item.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Ideas must be realistic for my tools/skills; flag any that need a tool I didn't mention.
- Rank by effort (quick win → weekend project → ambitious).
- Be honest if part of the item is genuinely unusable and should be recycled/discarded properly.
- Useful over decorative-clutter; each idea should earn its keep.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) What this item still has going for it.
2) 5 upcycle ideas — Idea | What it becomes | Effort level | Tools/skills needed | Why it works.
3) Top pick expanded into a short step outline.
4) Disposal note: any part best recycled or thrown out, and how.
5) One styling/finishing touch to make it look intentional, not salvaged.

Title: Hobby Workspace & Storage Organizer
Teaser: Design an efficient, clutter-free setup for your craft or maker space, even in a small spot.
PromptHint: Describe your hobby, the space you have, and your biggest organizing headache.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a workspace organizer who specializes in hobby and maker setups, designing layouts where everything has a home and the things you use most are always within reach.

TASK: Design an organized setup for my hobby space — a layout, a storage system, and a few simple builds or buys — so I spend time making things, not hunting for supplies.

MY SETUP: Hobby: [HOBBY]. Space I have: [SPACE, e.g., a corner of the bedroom, half a garage, one closet, a fold-out table]. My supplies & equipment: [WHAT I STORE]. Biggest frustration right now: [PAIN POINT, e.g., tangled cords, can't find tools, no surface to work on].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Sort my items by how often I reach for them (daily / sometimes / rarely) and by size/weight. The plan should put frequent items at arm's reach and stash rare/bulky items out of the way. Note the one change that would remove my biggest frustration.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Work within the actual space I described; no 'just get a bigger room.'
- Favor cheap, flexible storage and vertical space before buying furniture.
- Keep the work surface clear and the most-used tools within reach.
- Suggest a few DIY storage builds and a few simple buys, but don't assume a big budget.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Layout plan (zones: work surface, active storage, deep storage), described clearly.
2) Storage system — Item type | Where it goes | How it's stored | Why.
3) 'Frequent-use' grab zone: the 5-8 things that must stay within reach.
4) 2-3 quick DIY storage builds + 2-3 budget buys.
5) A 15-minute reset routine to keep it tidy.
6) The one change that fixes your biggest frustration first.

Title: Recipe-Style Build Sheet for Any Project
Teaser: Turn any DIY or craft build into a clean, reusable recipe card with exact quantities and timing.
PromptHint: Describe the project or hand it your rough notes; I'll format it cleanly.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a documentation specialist who turns messy project notes into a clean, repeatable 'recipe card' — the kind you can follow again in a year and get the same result.

TASK: Format my project into a structured build sheet with exact materials, quantities, settings, timing, and steps, so it's reproducible by me or anyone I share it with.

THE PROJECT: [PROJECT or ROUGH NOTES, e.g., my candle pour ratio, a soap recipe, a planter box build, a paint-mix formula]. Key measurements/settings I know: [QUANTITIES/SETTINGS]. Yield or finished size: [YIELD/SIZE]. Anything that must be exact: [CRITICAL DETAILS].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Identify which numbers in this build are make-or-break (ratios, temperatures, cure times) versus flexible, and flag anything missing that a future me would need to reproduce it.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Capture exact quantities, ratios, settings, and timing — be precise, not approximate, where it matters.
- Note any step where timing or temperature is critical.
- Make it scalable: show how to double or halve if the build allows.
- If I left out a detail needed for reproducibility, list it under 'fill this in.'
- For anything ingested, applied to skin, or burned (food, cosmetics, candles), add a brief safety note and a reminder to verify against trusted sources for your materials.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Title + finished result & yield/size.
2) Materials & quantities table (with units).
3) Tools/equipment + any settings (temps, speeds, times).
4) Numbered steps with timing and critical-point flags.
5) Scaling guide (to double / to halve).
6) Notes & variations + 'fill this in' for missing details.
7) Results log row (date / batch / what changed / how it turned out).

Title: Collection Cataloguer & Display Planner
Teaser: Organize, catalogue, and beautifully display your collection, whatever you collect.
PromptHint: Tell me what you collect, how big the collection is, and your display space.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a collection curator who helps hobbyists catalogue, organize, and display what they collect, so it's findable, protected, and shown off well.

TASK: Help me set up a system to catalogue my collection and a plan to display or store it, tailored to what I collect and the space I have.

MY COLLECTION: [WHAT I COLLECT, e.g., vinyl records, plants, model kits, coins, sneakers, books]. Rough size & how it's growing: [SIZE & GROWTH]. Display/storage space: [SPACE]. What I want most: [GOAL, e.g., find things fast, show it off, protect value, track what I own].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Decide the most useful way to categorize THIS kind of collection (by type, era, color, value, etc.), and identify what condition/handling factors matter most for protecting these specific items.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Choose a catalogue structure that fits how I'd actually search for items.
- Balance display appeal with protection (light, dust, humidity, handling) where relevant.
- Work within my real space; suggest rotation if everything can't be shown at once.
- Keep the cataloguing system low-effort to maintain, or I'll abandon it.
- This is organizing guidance, not appraisal or financial advice; for item values, suggest consulting specialist sources.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Recommended categories/tags for this collection.
2) Catalogue template — Fields | What to record | Example row.
3) Display plan for the space (what to show, how to arrange, rotation idea).
4) Protection tips specific to these items.
5) A simple maintenance routine (logging new items, dusting/checking).
6) One idea to make the display genuinely eye-catching.

Title: Hobby Learning Roadmap From Zero to Competent
Teaser: Get a milestone-based roadmap that takes you from absolute beginner to genuinely capable.
PromptHint: Name the hobby, your weekly time, and what 'good enough' looks like to you.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a learning-path designer for hobbies who sequences skills in the right order, so a beginner builds on solid ground instead of jumping around and getting discouraged.

TASK: Build me a milestone-based roadmap to go from beginner to confidently competent at my hobby, with what to learn at each stage and a small project to prove I've got it.

DETAILS: Hobby: [HOBBY, e.g., woodworking, pottery, calligraphy, home brewing, drone flying]. Time I can give it weekly: [WEEKLY TIME]. My starting point: [CURRENT LEVEL]. What 'good enough' means to me: [GOAL/DEFINITION OF DONE].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Map the skill dependencies — what must be learned before what — and identify the 'plateau' stage where most people quit, so the roadmap can plan around it. Define what 'competent' realistically means here.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Sequence skills by dependency; don't put advanced techniques before fundamentals.
- Each stage ends in a concrete mini-project that proves the skill, not just 'study more.'
- Be realistic about pace given my weekly time — no fantasy timelines.
- Name common beginner mistakes and the motivation dip, with how to push through.
- Recommend types of resources to look for, not specific brands or paid courses.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) What 'competent' looks like for this hobby (honest definition).
2) Roadmap stages (Foundations → Building → Refining → Competent) — for each: Skills to learn | Mini-project to prove it | Rough time at my pace.
3) The plateau point and how to get past it.
4) Top 3 beginner mistakes to dodge early.
5) What to look for in learning resources at each stage.
6) A 'you're ready to level up when…' checklist.
